summary: DHH argues that persuasion is a long-term process, not an instant conversion event. Most arguments don't immediately change minds but leave cracks in people's cognitive defenses that widen over time. He draws on two decades of writing and debating online to observe that patient argumentation is a powerful force. He also notes that the act of changing someone's mind can forge deep friendships, but only if the conversion is met with grace rather than scorn.
key_points: Arguments should not be judged by whether they immediately convert someone — there's a long tail of persuasion
Even the best arguments rarely breach mental walls instantly, but they leave cracks that time and reflection can widen
Changing minds is painful, but it creates opportunities for enduring bonds and friendships
Grace in receiving someone's changed perspective is essential — gloating or contempt makes any conversion brittle
money_quotes: Even when the best arguments fail to breach the mental walls, they often leave cracks. Cracks which time, new observations, and deeper consideration can open.
"Right now, right here" is a bad timeframe to measure the success of your arguments on. There's a long tail of conversion.
Patient persuasion is none the less a mighty force that does move minds.
Most of my best friends are those who've changed my mind, and I theirs.
The grace of simply being happy that someone heard what you had to say, and eventually found it compelling. Who cares how long it took, just be happy that they're here now.
Any conversion in perspective will be brittle if it's greeted with scorn and contempt. Whether I TOLD YOU SO or WHAT TOOK YOU SO LONG.
key_insight: Persuasion works on a long timeline — arguments leave cracks that eventually crumble walls, and the process deserves patience and grace rather than triumphalism.
